title: Advance Directives and Advance Care Planning
subtitle: A capacitated 74-year-old with chronic illness wants to complete her advance directives on admission. The nurse explains the document types, facilitates her choices, names a proxy, and ensures the directive is honored.

patient.name: Frances Whitaker
patient.admitDate: Medical unit, admitted with a chronic illness. Alert and oriented with full decision-making capacity. Wishes to complete advance directives and name a healthcare proxy. Nurse facilitating advance care planning.

vitals.temp: Names her daughter as healthcare proxy
Directive in chart; team and proxy informed

labs.r: Not decide or give legal advice.
If no proxy and capacity lost.

nursesNote: Frances Whitaker, 74, capacitated, completing advance directives on admission. Advance directives generally take effect only when the patient loses decision-making capacity (except a POLST/DNR, which are active provider orders). A living will states which life-sustaining treatments she wants or refuses; a durable power of attorney for healthcare (healthcare proxy) names a surrogate who uses substituted judgment. She can change or revoke a directive anytime while she has capacity. The nurse facilitates and educates (not gives legal advice or decides for her), ensures the documents are witnessed per policy and placed in the record and communicated to the team, and advocates for her wishes to be honored.
providerOrders: Offer advance care planning; provide advance directive information and resources
Encourage designation of a healthcare proxy and discussion of wishes
Ensure completed directives are witnessed per policy and placed in the record
Communicate the directive to the care team; honor the patient's wishes
Reassess and update as the patient's condition or wishes change

opts.text: Advance care planning lets patients state their wishes before a crisis
A living will specifies which life-sustaining treatments a patient wants or refuses
A durable power of attorney for healthcare names a surrogate decision-maker
Most advance directives take effect only when the patient loses capacity
A capacitated patient gives up the right to decide once a directive exists
A POLST is a provider-signed medical order that is active across care settings
A healthcare proxy/agent uses substituted judgment (what the patient would want)
A capacitated patient can change or revoke a directive at any time
The nurse facilitates and educates but does not give legal advice or decide for the patient
Directives should be documented in the record and communicated to the team
A nurse who is the named agent should also witness the document
If there is no directive and the patient loses capacity, a surrogate hierarchy applies
Assess the patient's understanding and wishes about future care
Provide information on advance directive types and the proxy role
Encourage naming a proxy and discussing wishes with them/family
Ensure documents are completed, witnessed per policy, and in the record
Communicate the directive to the team and reassess over time; document
Written instructions about which life-sustaining treatments you want or refuse
States your end-of-life wishes for terminal illness or permanent unconsciousness
Names a person to make health decisions for you if you cannot
The designated agent uses substituted judgment on your behalf
Covers decisions in situations a living will didn't specifically address
A provider-signed medical order that is portable across care settings
An immediately actionable order such as do-not-resuscitate

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Confirm the patient has capacity and wants to discuss advance care planning
Educate on living wills, the healthcare proxy, and POLST
Support the patient's own choices without influencing them
Place the completed directive in the chart and notify the team
Reinforce that it can be changed anytime; document
Provide information and resources about advance directives
Encourage naming a proxy and discussing wishes with them
Ensure the completed directive is placed in the medical record
Honor the patient's right to change or revoke the directive anytime
Confirm a capacitated patient still makes her own decisions
Complete the advance directive for the patient and choose for her
Pressure the patient toward a specific choice
Ignore the directive once the patient loses capacity
